danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites scattered throughout platform terrain
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player safety; collision triggers damage

danger_signals.visual_form: Switches, keys, and environmental hazards positioned in level layouts
danger_signals.meaning: Mechanical/environmental danger; failure to solve blocks progression and may cause damage

safety_signals.visual_form: Dagger and spiral blade items scattered in levels
safety_signals.meaning: Player empowerment; acquiring weapons increases combat capability and safety

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual forms (phoenix bird, frog, fish) available in specific levels
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability or level-specific safety through form change; phoenix gains flight, frog gains jumping, fish gains swimming

safety_signals.visual_form: Return to map level after finishing a stage
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone; player returns to navigation screen between challenges

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Jungle setting with vegetation; levels organized by geographical progression on map

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple monster types populate each level with varied behaviors
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout jungle environments as ambient threats

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Traditional side-scrolling map level with branching paths; 14 levels plus bonus levels linked by visual map interface
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must complete levels sequentially to unlock new areas; map-gating ensures progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: No map level present; 19 consecutive levels with linear progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player moves through consecutive underground levels without map navigation; continuous forward momentum required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Overhead-view map level (significant visual departure from episodes 1-2); different navigation paradigm
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player navigates from top-down perspective; strategic planning of route differs from side-scrolling episodes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition from active level to map screen; passive ending level sequences
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player loses control during passive finish levels; narrative closure before returning to map navigation

environmental_storytelling.example: Episode 1 uses branching map layout to show interconnected jungle areas; Episode 2 underground sequence implies descent; Episode 3 overhead map suggests expanded view of world
Flying phoenix appears in specific levels (sky/tall areas implied); frog in jumping-challenge levels; fish in water/swimming sections—environment dictates available abilities
Keys and switches positioned to require player exploration of level space; finding solutions communicates level geometry and environmental purpose
Conclusion sequences where player has no control visually communicate story resolution without gameplay interaction

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can fire collected weapons (dagger, spiral blade) at enemies; offensive capability active
Flies through levels; vertical movement available; implies sky-based threat or navigation challenge
Jumps with enhanced capability; implies jumping-puzzle or platform-challenge level type
Swims through water; implies aquatic level section or water-based navigation
Populate levels as ambient threats; require combat engagement or avoidance; visual presence signals danger

notes: Jill of the Jungle uses a tri-episode structure with distinct visual paradigms: Episode 1 uses traditional side-scrolling map navigation, Episode 2 removes the map (linear progression), and Episode 3 shifts to overhead view—each visual shift communicates world-state progression. Danger is communicated through enemy presence and puzzle obstacles; safety through weapons, transformations, and level completion checkpoints. The game lacks explicit faction conflict or political systems; instead uses environmental storytelling and level topology to communicate progression through distinct world regions (jungle → underground → final areas). Transformation mechanics serve as both safety signals and environmental communication—available transformations indicate level type and expected challenges.
